title: How Gold Deposits Form in the Earth

lesson.learn:
Gold does not usually appear as giant pure lumps waiting underground. It is often concentrated by hot fluids, pressure changes, magmatic processes, and later erosion. Hydrothermal systems can move dissolved gold through cracks and veins, then deposit it when temperature, pressure, or chemistry changes. Later, weathering can concentrate the metal further in stream beds and placer deposits.

That is why gold geology is a story about movement. Gold is often found where Earth's fluids and rocks have done a lot of work over a long time.
lesson.simpler: Gold deposits form when hot fluids move gold through rock and later concentrate it in veins or sediment.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Gold is concentrated by geologic processes, not magically created in place.
lesson.deeperDive:
Primary gold deposits often form in quartz veins or altered rock around hydrothermal systems. Erosion can then break those veins apart and carry the gold downstream, where density helps concentrate it in placer deposits. That is why some gold is mined in hard rock and some is panned from gravel. In both cases, the ore is the result of concentration, transport, and separation.

Gold is valuable partly because Earth rarely concentrates it in high amounts. The planet makes tiny amounts available; geology has to gather them into something worth mining.
lesson.funFact: Most gold starts out scattered very thinly through rock.

people: Economic geologists - scientists who study ore bodies
Prospectors - people who search for deposits
Environmental scientists - people who study mining impacts
places: Quartz veins - common hosts for gold
River gravels - where placer gold accumulates
Old mines - places that reveal ore bodies
years: Present day - gold deposits are mapped with geoscience tools
concepts: Vein - a crack filled with minerals
Placer deposit - gold concentrated by water and gravity
Weathering - rock breakdown that frees gold
Ore - rock containing enough metal to mine
